name: Cloud-Native Application Design Principles
description: Designing stateless, containerized applications following 12-factor app methodology for portability, scalability, and resilience on Google Cloud.
key_facts: 12-factor app: codebase in version control, dependencies declared, config in environment, backing services as attached resources
Stateless services store session state externally (Memorystore, Firestore) enabling horizontal scaling
Health checks (liveness and readiness probes) in GKE enable Kubernetes to manage pod lifecycle correctly
Circuit breaker pattern (via Istio or Resilience4j) prevents cascading failures in microservices
Idempotency ensures repeated API calls produce the same result, critical for retry logic and at-least-once delivery
common_misconceptions: Containerizing an existing app makes it cloud-native — true cloud-native requires redesigning for statelessness, observability, and resilience
Stateless means no state — stateless services delegate state to external stores; they do not eliminate state

name: Deploying Workloads to GKE
description: Creating Kubernetes Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, and Ingress resources to run production applications on GKE.
key_facts: Deployments manage stateless workloads; StatefulSets manage stateful applications with stable network identity and persistent storage
ConfigMaps store non-sensitive configuration; Secrets store sensitive data (use with Secret Manager for GCP-native secrets)
Kubernetes Ingress exposes HTTP/S routes to services; GKE uses Google Cloud Load Balancer as the ingress controller
Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) scales pod replicas based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics via Cloud Monitoring
Rolling updates with maxUnavailable and maxSurge control deployment speed; kubectl rollout undo reverts deployments
common_misconceptions: Kubernetes Secrets are encrypted — base64-encoded by default, not encrypted; use KMS encryption for secrets at rest in GKE
LoadBalancer Service type is always needed — ClusterIP with Ingress is more efficient for HTTP/S; LoadBalancer is best for non-HTTP protocols

name: Cloud Run Service Deployment and Traffic Management
description: Deploying containerized services to Cloud Run with traffic splitting, revision management, custom domains, and environment configuration.
key_facts: Cloud Run revisions are immutable snapshots; traffic can be split between revisions for canary deployments
Environment variables and mounted secrets configure Cloud Run services; Secret Manager integration provides version control
Cloud Run services can be triggered by HTTP, Pub/Sub push, Cloud Scheduler, or Eventarc event sources
Cloud Run for Anthos extends serverless container deployment to on-premises and multi-cloud environments
Concurrency setting controls max simultaneous requests per container instance (default 80, max 1000)
common_misconceptions: Cloud Run only scales based on HTTP requests — it also scales based on Pub/Sub messages, Eventarc events, and Cloud Scheduler triggers
Cloud Run revisions are deleted when not receiving traffic — old revisions persist until explicitly deleted, allowing instant traffic restore

name: CI/CD Pipelines with Cloud Build and Cloud Deploy
description: Building automated CI/CD pipelines using Cloud Build for testing and building container images, and Cloud Deploy for release management.
key_facts: cloudbuild.yaml defines build steps as Docker containers; each step runs sequentially or in parallel using waitFor
Build triggers connect Cloud Build to source repositories and trigger on push, PR, or tag events
Cloud Deploy delivery pipelines define promotion sequences (dev -> staging -> prod) with approval gates
Skaffold defines how to build and deploy applications and is the build tool used by Cloud Deploy
Binary Authorization enforces container image signing policies before Cloud Deploy can deploy to GKE
common_misconceptions: Cloud Build and Cloud Deploy are alternatives — they are complementary; Build creates artifacts, Deploy manages promotion pipelines
Cloud Build runs only on GCP infrastructure — Cloud Build workers run in isolated ephemeral containers on GCP always

name: Pub/Sub Messaging and Event-Driven Patterns
description: Integrating applications with Pub/Sub for asynchronous messaging, including push vs pull subscriptions, dead letter topics, and message ordering.
key_facts: Pull subscriptions: consumers poll for messages and must acknowledge within acknowledgment deadline (default 10s)
Push subscriptions: Pub/Sub delivers messages to an HTTPS endpoint; simpler for Cloud Run and App Engine
Dead letter topics capture messages that fail delivery after configurable max delivery attempts
Message ordering: enable ordering key on publisher and subscription to deliver messages in order per key
Pub/Sub Lite is a zonal (not global) lower-cost alternative with manual partition management for high-volume, cost-sensitive workloads
common_misconceptions: Pub/Sub delivers messages exactly once — it guarantees at-least-once; use deduplication IDs or idempotent consumers for exactly-once
Unacknowledged messages are lost — unacknowledged messages are redelivered after the acknowledgment deadline expires

name: Cloud Functions Event-Driven Serverless Computing
description: Building event-driven serverless functions triggered by HTTP, Pub/Sub, Cloud Storage, Firestore, and other GCP events using Cloud Functions.
key_facts: Cloud Functions 2nd gen is built on Cloud Run, supports longer timeouts (up to 60 min) and more memory (up to 32 GB)
Trigger types: HTTP, Pub/Sub, Cloud Storage, Firestore, Firebase, Cloud Scheduler, Eventarc
Functions are stateless; use Firestore or Memorystore for state that must persist across invocations
Concurrency in 2nd gen: single function instance can handle multiple concurrent requests (unlike 1st gen)
Cold start latency can be reduced with minimum instances or using lighter runtimes (Node.js, Go over Java)
common_misconceptions: Cloud Functions and Cloud Run are completely different — Cloud Functions 2nd gen runs on Cloud Run infrastructure internally
Cloud Functions scale infinitely without limit — there are default concurrency and resource limits per project and region

name: Firestore NoSQL Document Database
description: Using Firestore as a serverless document database for web and mobile applications with real-time sync, offline support, and hierarchical data modeling.
key_facts: Firestore stores data as documents in collections; documents can contain nested subcollections for hierarchical data
Real-time listeners push database changes to connected clients instantly without polling
Security rules in Firestore (Native mode) control read/write access based on auth state and document fields
Firestore is available in two modes: Native (Firebase features, real-time) and Datastore mode (Datastore API compatibility)
Transactions and batched writes ensure atomic operations across multiple documents
common_misconceptions: You can run complex joins across collections like SQL — Firestore has no JOIN support; design data to be read in its stored form
Firestore Datastore mode and Native mode are interchangeable — once a database is created in a mode it cannot be changed

name: Application Testing Strategies for Cloud-Native Apps
description: Implementing unit, integration, and end-to-end testing strategies for cloud-native applications using GCP testing tools and local emulators.
key_facts: GCP emulators for local testing: Pub/Sub emulator, Firestore emulator, Cloud Storage emulator (fake-gcs-server)
Cloud Build can run test steps in parallel to speed up CI pipeline execution using waitFor: ['-']
Test-driven development (TDD) for cloud functions: unit tests mock GCP client libraries using sinon or unittest.mock
Integration testing against real GCP services uses test projects with cleanup teardown to avoid cost accumulation
Load testing with Cloud Tasks or Locust validates autoscaling behavior before production deployment
common_misconceptions: Cloud emulators are 100% compatible with real GCP services — emulators may not support all features or edge cases
Testing is only needed before deployment — continuous testing in production via canary deployments and synthetic monitoring is also essential

name: API Management with Cloud Endpoints and API Gateway
description: Exposing, securing, and managing APIs for microservices using Cloud Endpoints (OpenAPI/gRPC) and API Gateway with authentication and rate limiting.
key_facts: Cloud Endpoints uses Extensible Service Proxy (ESP) to enforce OpenAPI or gRPC service definitions on backend services
API Gateway is a fully managed, serverless alternative to Endpoints for managing REST APIs backed by Cloud Run or Cloud Functions
Authentication options: API keys, Firebase Auth JWT tokens, Google service account JWTs, and Auth0
Rate limiting and quotas protect backend services from traffic spikes and abuse
Apigee is enterprise-grade API management with analytics, developer portal, and monetization
common_misconceptions: Cloud Endpoints, API Gateway, and Apigee are interchangeable — they serve different scales: Endpoints (simple), API Gateway (serverless), Apigee (enterprise)
API keys provide authentication — API keys identify the calling application but do not authenticate the user identity

name: Caching with Memorystore (Redis and Memcached)
description: Implementing in-memory caching with fully managed Memorystore for Redis or Memcached to reduce latency and database load in cloud-native applications.
key_facts: Memorystore for Redis supports versions 6.x and 7.x with Standard tier for HA and replication across zones
Redis persistence (RDB snapshots or AOF) provides durability; Memcached is purely in-memory with no persistence
VPC peering is required to connect Cloud Run or GKE to Memorystore instances (no public IP access)
Redis is suitable for session storage, leaderboards, pub/sub, and complex data structures; Memcached for simple key-value caching
Cache-aside pattern: application checks cache first, reads from DB on miss, then populates cache for future requests
common_misconceptions: Redis and Memcached are interchangeable — Redis supports rich data types (lists, sets, sorted sets); Memcached is simpler key-value only
Memorystore is accessible from the public internet — it is VPC-only; applications must be in the same VPC or use VPC peering

name: Error Handling, Debugging, and Observability
description: Implementing structured logging, distributed tracing, error reporting, and profiling for deployed applications on GCP.
key_facts: Structured logging (JSON format) in GCP allows log-based metrics and alerting on specific log fields
Cloud Trace automatically instruments latency data for App Engine and Cloud Run; custom spans use OpenTelemetry SDK
Error Reporting groups and counts distinct errors from logs; integrates with Cloud Monitoring for alerting
Cloud Profiler continuously profiles CPU and heap usage of production applications with low overhead
Correlation IDs propagated across service calls enable distributed trace reconstruction in Cloud Trace
common_misconceptions: Logging to stdout is not sufficient on GCP — stdout/stderr is captured by Cloud Logging for Cloud Run and GKE automatically
Profiling always requires code changes — Cloud Profiler uses an agent-based approach with minimal changes for supported languages

name: Integrating Applications with Cloud SQL and Cloud Spanner
description: Connecting cloud-native applications to Cloud SQL (using connection pool and Cloud SQL Connector) and Cloud Spanner (using client libraries and sessions).
key_facts: Cloud SQL Auth Proxy (or Cloud SQL Connector) provides secure, IAM-authenticated connections without IP allowlisting
Connection pooling is critical for serverless environments; pgBouncer or built-in pool settings prevent connection exhaustion
Cloud Spanner client libraries use multiplexed sessions for efficient connection reuse across multiple requests
Spanner mutations vs DML: mutations are lower latency for single-row operations; DML supports SQL UPDATE/DELETE
For Cloud Run: mount the Cloud SQL socket using Cloud SQL Connector sidecar or use the UNIX domain socket
common_misconceptions: Cloud SQL connections do not need a proxy — direct IP connections require IP allowlisting; the Auth Proxy is the secure alternative
Cloud Spanner sessions are like database connections — sessions are lightweight; one session can multiplex many transactions

name: Security Best Practices for Cloud Developers
description: Implementing security in the development lifecycle: secret management, IAM for applications, vulnerability scanning, and secure dependency management.
key_facts: Secret Manager stores API keys, passwords, and certificates with versioning, rotation, and audit logging
Never hardcode secrets in code or Docker images; use Secret Manager or environment variables injected at runtime
Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning scans container images for CVEs using Container Analysis
Workload Identity Federation allows external identities (GitHub Actions, AWS) to access GCP without service account keys
Principle of least privilege: Cloud Run service accounts should only have the specific roles needed for that service
common_misconceptions: Environment variables are a secure place to store secrets — env vars can be read by any process and may appear in logs; use Secret Manager
Container images from public registries are safe to use — always scan base images for vulnerabilities with Artifact Analysis

name: Application Scaling and Performance Optimization
description: Configuring autoscaling for GKE and Cloud Run, optimizing cold start latency, and using Cloud CDN and global load balancing for performance.
key_facts: Cloud Run min-instances prevents cold starts; max-instances caps resource consumption and cost
GKE Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) adjusts CPU/memory requests; HPA adjusts replica count; cluster autoscaler adjusts node count
Cloud CDN caches static assets at edge PoPs; Cache-Control headers control TTL for individual resources
KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) scales GKE workloads based on Pub/Sub queue depth or custom metrics
Latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99) provide better performance insight than averages for tail latency optimization
common_misconceptions: Horizontal scaling always solves performance problems — some workloads are CPU-bound on a single request; vertical scaling or algorithm optimization is needed
Cloud CDN caches all content automatically — only cacheable responses (correct Cache-Control headers, GET/HEAD methods) are cached

name: Cloud Storage Integration for Applications
description: Integrating Cloud Storage into application workflows for object uploads, signed URLs, notifications, and efficient large object handling.
key_facts: Signed URLs grant time-limited upload/download access to private objects without user authentication
Resumable uploads are recommended for files over 5 MB to handle network interruptions gracefully
Cloud Storage FUSE mounts buckets as file systems on Compute Engine or GKE pods for ML training workloads
Object change notifications via Pub/Sub or Eventarc trigger Cloud Functions or Cloud Run on object creation/deletion
Parallel composite uploads split large files for faster upload by uploading parts simultaneously and composing them
common_misconceptions: Cloud Storage requires authentication for all access — public buckets and signed URLs enable unauthenticated access for specific use cases
FUSE mounting works well for high-throughput random reads — Cloud Storage FUSE is optimized for sequential reads; use local SSD for random I/O
